data.q:
A 55-year-old male with a history of hypertension and smoking presents to the Emergency Department complaining of severe, crushing substernal chest pain that started 1 hour ago while gardening. The pain radiates to his left jaw and shoulder. He reports associated nausea and diaphoresis.

On physical examination:
BP: 150/95 mmHg, HR: 104 bpm, RR: 22/min, O2 Sat: 96% on RA.
Cardiac auscultation reveals an S4 gallop but no murmurs. Lungs are clear to auscultation.

ECG reveals ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL.
data.diagnosis:
Diagnosis: Acute Inferior Wall Myocardial Infarction (STEMI).

Explanation & Plan:
1. The ECG findings (ST elevation in II, III, aVF) are classic for an inferior wall MI, which involves the Right Coronary Artery (RCA) in 80% of cases.
2. Immediate Management: Activate PCI team (Door-to-balloon time < 90 mins). Administer Aspirin (300mg chewed) and a P2Y12 inhibitor (e.g., Clopidogrel/Ticagrelor).
3. Caution: Nitrates should be used with extreme caution or avoided in inferior MI until right ventricular involvement is ruled out, as they can cause severe hypotension due to preload reduction.
